title: When K-Means Struggles
concept: K-Means is sensitive to where its centroids start, assumes clusters are roughly round and similar in size, and can badly misread data with oddly shaped or uneven groups.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the elbow method help you choose?

options.text: A reasonable value for K, the number of clusters
Which data points are outliers
How fast the algorithm should run

exercises.explain: Right! The elbow method helps pick a reasonable K by spotting diminishing returns.

cards.title: Same puzzle, different starting move
cards.body: Two people solve the same jigsaw puzzle, but one starts from a corner and the other starts from the middle. They might both finish, but they take very different paths — and sometimes one gets stuck in a worse arrangement along the way. K-Means has that same problem with where it starts.

cards.title: Sensitivity to starting centroids
cards.body: K-Means usually starts by placing its K centroids at random positions. Because the assign-and-update loop only ever adjusts from there, a bad random start can lock the algorithm into a worse final grouping — different random starts can produce noticeably different results on the exact same data.

cards.title: Rolling downhill, not searching the whole map
cards.body: K-Means behaves like a ball rolling downhill from wherever it's dropped — it settles into the nearest valley, not necessarily the deepest valley on the entire map. Drop it in a different spot (a different random start) and it may roll into a shallower, less ideal valley instead.

cards.title: When shapes break the assumption
cards.body: Picture data shaped like two concentric rings, one inside the other, like a donut. K-Means assumes clusters are roughly round blobs of similar size, so with K=2 it tends to slice the rings in half rather than separating inner ring from outer ring — the true shape doesn't match what K-Means is built to find.

cards.title: It's not broken — it's just built for round, even blobs
cards.body: You might think K-Means failing on odd shapes means something is wrong with the algorithm. In reality, K-Means is working exactly as designed — it's built around distance-to-a-center, which naturally favors roughly circular, similar-sized clusters. Oddly shaped or very unevenly sized groups simply fall outside what that design handles well.

cards.title: Where K-Means shines vs. struggles
cards.body: The same algorithm, two very different fits:

right.text:
Rings, crescents, or
wildly uneven cluster sizes

exercises.prompt: Why is K-Means considered sensitive to its starting centroid positions?

options.text: Because a bad random start can cause the algorithm to settle into a worse final grouping
Because it changes the value of K automatically depending on the start
Because starting positions determine how many data points exist
Because it refuses to run unless centroids start in the exact center of the data

exercises.explain: Correct! Since K-Means only ever adjusts from wherever it starts, a poor random start can lock in a worse result.

exercises.prompt: True or false: K-Means tends to work best when clusters are roughly round and similar in size.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! K-Means' distance-to-center approach naturally favors round, evenly-sized clusters.

exercises.sentence: K-Means can produce different results on the same data depending on where its centroids ___.
exercises.explain: Different random starting positions can lead the algorithm to settle into different final groupings.

exercises.prompt: A dataset has two crescent-moon shaped clusters curving around each other. Which statement about applying K-Means here is accurate?

options.text: K-Means will likely struggle, since it assumes roughly round clusters and crescents aren't round
K-Means will perfectly separate the crescents every time, regardless of shape
K-Means cannot run at all on curved data

exercises.explain: Right! K-Means' round-blob assumption makes it a poor fit for crescent-shaped clusters.

exercises.prompt: A data scientist runs K-Means three times on the same dataset with the same K, but gets three slightly different groupings each time. What's the most likely explanation?

options.text: Each run used different random starting centroid positions, which can lead to different final results
The dataset must have changed between each run
K-Means is broken and should never be used
K silently changed value between runs

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since starting centroids are usually random, different runs can settle into different final groupings — a known K-Means limitation.

exercises.prompt: A biologist has data forming one huge, dense cluster of common species and one tiny, sparse cluster of rare species. They run K-Means with K=2 and it splits the huge cluster awkwardly in half instead of separating common from rare. What's the best explanation?

options.text: K-Means assumes clusters are similarly sized and shaped, so it struggles when one true cluster is much bigger or denser than another
K-Means always ignores the largest cluster in a dataset
The biologist chose an invalid value of K
K-Means requires every cluster to contain the exact same number of points

exercises.explain: Exactly! K-Means' assumption of similarly-sized, round clusters causes it to mishandle groups with very different sizes or densities. Next: seeing where K-Means genuinely shines in the real world!
